title: Challenges in Existing Methods & Their Solutions
subtitle: How people cheat, what stops them, and what's still missing

data.title: The illusion of security
data.content:
Current systems produce records that cannot be completely trusted without additional safeguards implemented. Centralized databases present a single point of failure: a successful breach endangers the entire dataset at once, and records can be modified or erased without traceable evidence.

Token-based and forms-based methods carry a similar weakness, where users can share their credentials for proxy submissions without triggering any alert in the system.

data.title: Ways people cheat attendance
data.subtitle: Tap to flip the card and reveal the attack impact

cards.title: Buddy Punching
cards.front_desc: A colleague physically checks in on someone else's behalf using shared credentials.
cards.back_desc: The system logs a valid check-in. The absent teacher is recorded as present. No biometric = no barrier.

cards.title: Impersonation
cards.front_desc: An attacker illicitly obtains another person's ID, PIN, or access card and authenticates at the terminal.
cards.back_desc: The system successfully authenticates the attacker as the victim. The victim's identity is compromised; records show them present when they weren't.

cards.title: Presentation Attacks
cards.front_desc: An attacker holds a printed photograph or video replay in front of a biometric sensor.
cards.back_desc: Without robust presentation attack detection (PAD), the system may accept the 2D photo as a real face. The biometric system is bypassed using a simple artifact.

cards.title: Faking Timestamps
cards.front_desc: An employee manipulates the device clock or intercepts network traffic before checking in.
cards.back_desc: The falsified timestamp is submitted to the server. Without blockchain's immutable ledger, the timestamp is trusted blindly and the check-in is accepted.

cards.title: Record Tampering
cards.front_desc: An insider with database access maliciously overwrites attendance records.
cards.back_desc: Absences are changed to present. In a centralized system without blockchain, there is no audit trail and no proof of tampering can be produced.

data.title: Attack → solution mapping
data.subtitle: Blue = biometric solution, Violet = blockchain solution

data.title: Why biometrics are not enough
data.content:
Biometrics raise the bar for impersonation, but they do not eliminate it. Attackers can use printed photographs and video replay attacks to deceive sensors. While presentation attack detection (PAD) mechanisms mitigate this, they often fail against attacks captured under different environmental conditions.

Machine learning has improved overall matching accuracy, and multimodal architectures take this further by requiring simultaneous successful presentation across two or more biometric modalities. However, each additional modality expands the attack surface proportionally. Because biometric traits are permanent and physiologically unchangeable, the compromise of a single template carries an indefinite and irrevocable privacy risk.

data.title: Fusion strategies
data.subtitle: Select a fusion level to see where the two biometric streams are merged

levels.name: Sensor-level
levels.description: Raw sensor data from both modalities is combined before any feature extraction. Rarely used due to incompatible signal types, but maximally preserves information.

levels.name: Feature-level
levels.description: Feature vectors extracted from both modalities are concatenated into a joint representation before matching. Highest accuracy potential, but requires compatible feature spaces and higher compute.

levels.name: Score-level
levels.description: Each modality independently produces a match score; scores are combined via sum, product, or max rules. Most common — balances accuracy and modularity.

levels.name: Decision-level
levels.description: Each modality makes its own binary accept/reject decision; a majority vote or AND/OR rule produces the final result. Most modular, lowest compute.

data.title: Privacy-preservation techniques
data.subtitle: Tap a technique to see an animated explainer

techniques.title: Cancelable biometrics
techniques.subtitle: Irreversible transform of raw template
techniques.description: The raw biometric feature vector is passed through an irreversible, key-dependent transform (e.g. 3D Jigsaw, Secure Triplet Loss). If compromised, the key is revoked and a new template is generated — the original biometric is never exposed.

techniques.title: GAN synthetic templates
techniques.subtitle: Generative protection at storage
techniques.description: A GAN's generator learns to produce biometric templates statistically indistinguishable from real ones. These synthetic templates replace raw templates in storage — if stolen, they cannot be reverse-engineered to recover the real biometric.

techniques.title: Zero-knowledge proofs
techniques.subtitle: Prove identity without revealing data
techniques.description: The user proves they possess a valid biometric credential without revealing the actual template. Using Groth16 or zkSNARK, the verifier receives cryptographic proof of identity — zero private data crosses the wire.

techniques.title: Homomorphic encryption
techniques.subtitle: Match in encrypted domain
techniques.description: Biometric templates are encrypted using a homomorphic encryption scheme (e.g. BFV, CKKS). The server performs matching directly on the ciphertext — the plaintext template is never decrypted server-side.

techniques.title: Cryptographic key binding
techniques.subtitle: Fuzzy commitment / extractor
techniques.description: A fuzzy commitment or fuzzy extractor binds a cryptographic key to the biometric template at enrollment. At verification, the noisy biometric re-derives the same key if it matches.

data.title: A tension of properties
data.content:
To address the record integrity problem at its root, researchers have explored blockchain as an alternative to centralized databases. Because each block cryptographically links to the one before it, any attempt to modify information becomes immediately detectable across the distributed network. This completely mitigates the single point of failure risk.

However, the immutability property of blockchain conflicts directly with the permanence characteristic of biometric traits. Any biometric commitment linked on-chain remains permanently accessible, even if the underlying biometric record is later compromised.

data.title: The structural gap in revocation
data.content:
Self-sovereign identity (SSI) frameworks and decentralized identifiers (DIDs) allow users to retain mathematical sovereignty over their identity. Verifiable credentials (VCs) then allow issuers to attach signed assertions, such as attendance records, to a user's DID, complete with on-chain status mechanisms for revocation.

However, these revocation mechanisms fall short of enforcing revocation in any structural sense. On-chain status flags operate merely as verifier-side policy checks. They do not prevent the biometric pipeline from executing against the revoked identity's template, because the template's storage address remains independent of the DID and locatable regardless of credential state.

data.title: Current limitations
data.subtitle: Key gaps in existing combined biometric + blockchain systems

items.title: No integrated cancelable transforms
items.description: Multimodal biometric systems still expose raw templates — cancelable transformation mechanisms are rarely built in from the start.

items.title: Auditability vs. privacy trade-off unsolved
items.description: Blockchain provides immutability but lacks a reliable protocol for anchoring cancelable templates without compromising biometric privacy.

items.title: Incomplete identity revocation support
items.description: Smart contracts, DIDs, and VCs do not yet fully support coordinated revocation and re-issuance across all participating nodes.

items.title: On-site + remote gap remains unproven
items.description: The combined effectiveness of cancelable biometrics, blockchain, and DID/VC management across both deployment modes remains unestablished.
